## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in s(t) = I cos(ωt) − Q sin(ωt)** — This affects the phase convention; examiners will deduct 1 mark if the I-Q sign convention is inconsistent.
> 2. **Confusing E_s and E_b** — Many students write P_b = Q(√(E_s/N₀)) instead of Q(√(2E_b/N₀)). Always substitute E_s = 2E_b for QPSK.
> 3. **Drawing the constellation without labeling the dibits and phases** — KTU expects **all four points labeled** with their (I, Q) coordinates AND phase angles.
> 4. **Missing the carrier recovery block** in the receiver diagram. A QPSK receiver **without** carrier recovery is **incomplete** for 2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong symbol rate** — Writing R_s = R_b instead of R_s = R_b/2. This is a very common 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping the noise variance derivation** in BER problems. Show that var(n_I) = N₀/2 explicitly.
> 7. **Not stating the orthogonality condition** ∫φ₁(t)φ₂(t)dt = 0. This is the reason QPSK works — losing 1 mark for omitting it.
> 8. **Mixing up OQPSK and QPSK** — Standard QPSK allows 180° phase transitions (zero-crossings); OQPSK offsets Q by T_s/2 to avoid this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — QPSK & Signal Constellations**

- ✅ **QPSK transmits 2 bits per symbol** using four carrier phases: **π/4, 3π/4, 5π/4, 7π/4**
- ✅ **Bits per symbol (k) = log₂M = 2** for QPSK (M = 4)
- ✅ **Symbol rate R_s = R_b / 2** (half the bit rate)
- ✅ **Symbol duration T_s = 2 T_b** (twice the bit duration)
- ✅ **Bandwidth B = R_s = R_b/2** (half of BPSK)
- ✅ **Spectral efficiency η = 2 bits/s/Hz** (double BPSK)
- ✅ **Energy per bit E_b = E_s / 2**
- ✅ **BER formula: P_b = Q(√(2E_b/N₀))** — **identical to BPSK**
- ✅ **Minimum constellation distance d_min = √(2E_s) = 2√E_b**
- ✅ **Orthogonality:** I(t) and Q(t) modulated on cos and sin carriers do not interfere
- ✅ **Gray coding** is used to minimize bit errors from single symbol errors
- ✅ **Transmitter needs:** S/P converter, level converters, two balanced modulators, summer
- ✅ **Receiver needs:** Two correlators, integrate-and-dump filters, samplers, decision devices, **carrier recovery (Costas/squaring loop)**, P/S converter
- ✅ **Phase transitions in standard QPSK:** ±90°, ±180° (180° causes envelope zero-crossing)
- ✅ **Constant envelope property** makes QPSK robust to nonlinear amplification
- ✅ **Used in 4G LTE, Wi-Fi, DVB-S2, satellite links, cable modems**
- ✅ **Draw constellation as 4 points at corners of a square** with axes I and Q
- ✅ **Carrier recovery is MANDATORY** for coherent QPSK detection
- ✅ **AWGN is the standard channel model** for QPSK BER derivations
- ✅ **BER vs SNR curve for QPSK is same as BPSK** — same Q-function
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** More complex receiver; sensitive to phase noise; carrier recovery needed

> 🎯 **Most-tested KTU topics from this module (high-yield):**
> 1. QPSK transmitter/receiver block diagram (7-8 mark questions)
> 2. Constellation diagram with Gray coding (3-4 marks)
> 3. Derivation of P_b = Q(√(2E_b/N₀)) (full 14-mark question)
> 4. Bandwidth comparison: B_QPSK = B_BPSK / 2 (3-4 marks)
> 5. Waveform sketching from binary input (7 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plots of BER vs SNR with analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT744 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Plots of BER vs SNR with analysis # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is BER? **Bit Error Rate (BER)** is the fundamental performance metric of any digital communication system. It is formally defined as the ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted over a given observation interval. $$P_b = \frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total number of bits transmitted}}$$ In the probabilistic (ensemble-average) sense used by KTU examiners, BER converges to the **average probability of bit error**, denoted $P_b$ or $P_e$. Mathematically: $$P_b = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N_{errors}}{N_{bits}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** BER is the *expected probability* that a single transmitted bit will be incorrectly decoded at the receiver due to the combined effect of **AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise)**, channel distortion, and synchronization imperfections. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from **0** (perfect channel) to **0.5** (random guessing for binary schemes). ## 1.2 What is SNR and Why Eb/N0? In analog systems, we use **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** = $S/N$ measured at the receiver input. In digital communication, however, we normalize SNR to the **energy per information bit** because: - Different schemes transmit at different rates and use different bandwidths. - We need a **fair, modulation-independent metric** for comparison. The KTU-preferred metric is: $$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{\text{Average energy per information bit}}{\text{Noise power spectral density (W/Hz)}}$$ The conversion from ordinary SNR to $E_b/N_0$ is: $$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$$ where $B$ is the noise-equivalent bandwidth and $R_b$ is the bit rate. Since decibels are heavily used in KTU plots: $$\left[\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right]_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When a question states "SNR per bit", it ALWAYS means $E_b/N_0$ expressed in dB. This is the abscissa (x-axis) of every KTU BER plot you will draw. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Whispered Secret in a Party Imagine two friends exchanging whispered secrets at a loud party: - **Signal (S):** The whispered message amplitude — louder whisper → stronger signal. - **Noise (N):** The party chatter overlapping with the whisper. - **SNR = S/N:** How clearly the secret is heard relative to background noise. - **BER:** How often a word is misheard and decoded wrongly. If the whisper is **very loud** (high $E_b/N_0$), almost nothing is misheard — the BER curve falls off a cliff. If the whisper is **just barely louder than noise** (low $E_b/N_0$), the listener guesses half the time, and BER stays near **0.5** (a horizontal floor). The transition from "guessing" to "near-perfect" decoding is the famous **waterfall region** of the BER curve. Now imagine **two different whisper methods**: 1. **BPSK** — friend whispers only two distinct tones (0 or 1) far apart in pitch. Even a noisy ear can tell them apart. Power-efficient but slow. 2. **BFSK** — friend uses two close tones; you need to hear them separately. Easier to confuse, but you can pack more whispers per second. The BER vs $E_b/N_0$ plot is essentially the **"loudness-efficiency signature"** of each whisper method. ## 1.4 Engineering Relevance BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curves are the **"score cards"** of digital modulation. They are used in: - **Link budget design** for satellite, 4G/5G, and Wi-Fi systems. - **Coding gain evaluation** — coded systems shift the curve leftward. - **Hardware selection** — choosing a modem IC for a given target BER (e.g., $10^{-5}$ for voice, $10^{-9}$ for data). - **Trade-off analysis** — power efficiency vs. bandwidth efficiency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Highlight:** The plots and their asymptotic analysis are *guaranteed* 14-mark questions. A student who cannot sketch the relative ordering of BPSK, QPSK, BFSK, and DPSK curves from memory will lose marks. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Q-Function — The Heart of Every BER Equation Almost all coherent-detection BER expressions reduce to a **Q-function** of some argument. The Q-function is the tail probability of a standard normal distribution: $$Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{x}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du$$ Equivalently, in terms of the **complementary error function** erfc(·): $$Q(x) = \frac{1}{2} \, \text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$ **Useful bounds used by examiners:** $$\frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}} \left(1 - \frac{1}{x^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right) \le Q(x) \le \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right) \quad \text{for } x > 0$$ ## 2.2 BER Expressions for Standard Binary Schemes Below is the **KTU master formula sheet** for all modulation schemes typically asked. These are the *closed-form* expressions evaluated over an AWGN channel. | Modulation Scheme | Detection Type | Exact BER $P_b$ | Asymptotic Form (large $E_b/N_0$) | |---|---|---|---| | **BPSK** | Coherent | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi E_b/N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | | **QPSK / OQPSK** | Coherent | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Same as BPSK | | **BFSK (orthogonal)** | Coherent | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi E_b/N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2 N_0}\right)$ | | **BFSK (orthogonal)** | Non-coherent | $\dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2 N_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2 N_0}\right)$ | | **DPSK** | Differential (non-coherent) | $\dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Observation:** The argument inside the Q-function is the *only thing that changes* across binary schemes. Compare $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ for BPSK with $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ for BFSK — the **factor of 2** inside the square root is precisely the source of the famous **3-dB power advantage of BPSK over coherent BFSK** at high SNR. ## 2.3 Conversion Between $E_b/N_0$ and $S/N$ For a transmitter with bit rate $R_b$ and a receiver noise-equivalent bandwidth $B$: $$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{S}{N} = \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{R_b}{B}$$ For **BPSK/QPSK**: $B \approx R_b$ for BPSK, $B \approx R_b/2$ for QPSK. For **BFSK**: $B = \Delta f + 2R_b$ approximately (Carson's rule for the IF filter). ## 2.4 Bandwidth-Power Trade-off A KTU-favorite concept. The two extremes are: - **BPSK / QPSK** → **Power-efficient** (lower $E_b/N_0$ needed for a target BER), but **bandwidth-inefficient** (BPSK = 0.5 bits/s/Hz; QPSK = 1 bit/s/Hz). - **M-ary FSK / M-ary PSK** → **Bandwidth-efficient** at the cost of more **power** required. > [!NOTE] > **Shannon's Limit:** For any code, $P_b \to 0$ is achievable iff $E_b/N_0 > \ln 2 \approx -1.59$ dB. The gap between a practical scheme and this limit is called the **coding/modulation gap** — a key KTU terminology. ## 2.5 The Waterfall Curve — Anatomy A typical $P_b$ vs $E_b/N_0$ (dB) plot has three regions: 1. **High-BER floor (left side):** $E_b/N_0 < 0$ dB. The signal is buried in noise. $P_b \to 0.5$ for binary schemes. No coding/modulation can help much here. 2. **Waterfall region (middle):** A very steep drop of BER as $E_b/N_0$ increases. This is the *operational* region. The slope here is what differentiates schemes. 3. **Saturation / coding-limited region (right side):** Curves flatten to a target BER (e.g., $10^{-5}$). Channel coding (FEC) is used to push the curve further leftward. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility In real-world systems, an engineer reads these plots to answer: - *"I have a satellite link with $E_b/N_0 = 9$ dB. Can I use QPSK and achieve $P_b < 10^{-5}$?"* - *"If the link margin drops by 2 dB, will the system still meet the BER target?"* - *"Should I switch from DPSK to BPSK to save 3 dB of power, or stick with the simpler DPSK receiver?"* These are exactly the kinds of numerical reasoning questions KTU examiners design. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: BER of Coherent BPSK over AWGN **Setup.** Binary bits $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ are mapped to antipodal signals $s_0(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ and $s_1(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$, where $\phi(t)$ is a unit-energy basis pulse of duration $T_b$. The received signal is: $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i \in \{0, 1\}$$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$ (two-sided). **Step 1 — Correlator output.** Projecting onto $\phi(t)$: $$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi(t)\,dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + z$$ The noise component $z$ is Gaussian with mean **0** and variance: $$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b}\phi^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Decision rule.** The optimal (ML) decision rule is: $$\hat{b}_k = 0 \;\text{if}\; y \ge 0, \quad \text{else} \; \hat{b}_k = 1$$ **Step 3 — Probability of error.** Assume $s_0 = +\sqrt{E_b}$ is sent. An error occurs when $y < 0$: $$P(e \mid s_0) = P\!\left(z < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \tfrac{N_0}{2}\right) < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$ Standardizing $z/\sigma_z$: $$P(e \mid s_0) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}(0,1) < -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ By symmetry $P(e \mid s_1) = P(e \mid s_0)$, so the average BER is: $$\boxed{\,P_b^{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** The argument $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ is the **distance** between the two signal points **divided by twice the noise standard deviation** — a clean geometric interpretation examiners love. ## 3.2 Derivation: BER of Coherent Orthogonal BFSK **Setup.** Two orthogonal basis functions $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$ with $\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1\phi_2\,dt = 0$ and each of unit energy. Signals are $s_0(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$, $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_2(t)$. **Step 1 — Two correlator outputs.** $$y_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \begin{cases}\sqrt{E_b} + z_1, & s_0\\ z_1, & s_1\end{cases}$$ $$y_2 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = \begin{cases}z_2, & s_0\\ \sqrt{E_b} + z_2, & s_1\end{cases}$$ with $z_1, z_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ independent. **Step 2 — Decision rule.** Decide $s_0$ if $y_1 > y_2$, else $s_1$. **Step 3 — Error probability assuming $s_0$ sent.** $$P(e \mid s_0) = P(y_2 > y_1) = P(z_2 - z_1 > \sqrt{E_b})$$ The difference $w = z_2 - z_1$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $\sigma_w^2 = N_0/2 + N_0/2 = N_0$. Standardizing: $$P(e \mid s_0) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}(0,1) > \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ By symmetry: $$\boxed{\,P_b^{BFSK,\,coh} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **3-dB Loss vs BPSK:** Compare the two arguments: $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ (BPSK) versus $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ (BFSK). At a target $P_b = 10^{-5}$ the BPSK requires roughly half the energy. Translating to dB: $10\log_{10}(2) \approx 3$ dB. **This is the canonical answer** for "Why does BPSK outperform BFSK by 3 dB at high SNR?". ## 3.3 Derivation: BER of Non-Coherent Orthogonal BFSK For non-coherent detection, the receiver forms envelope detector outputs $r_1 = \vert y_1 \vert$ and $r_2 = \vert y_2 \vert$ and chooses the larger. The conditional densities follow a **Rician distribution** when a signal is present, and **Rayleigh** when only noise is present. After integrating the appropriate Rician/Rayleigh densities, the closed-form error probability is: $$P_b^{BFSK,\,noncoh} = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$$ The factor of **1/2** in the exponent (vs $E_b/N_0$ for DPSK) is the source of the **additional 3-dB penalty** relative to BPSK — making non-coherent BFSK a *total 6-dB worse* than BPSK at high SNR. ## 3.4 BER of Differential BPSK (DPSK) DPSK encodes information in the **phase difference** between successive symbols. The optimal non-coherent demodulator correlates the current symbol with the previous one. The resulting BER is: $$P_b^{DPSK} = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$ Note that DPSK is **3 dB better** than non-coherent BFSK and only **about 1 dB worse** than coherent BPSK at high $E_b/N_0$, while needing *no carrier phase tracking* — the canonical KTU engineering trade-off question. ## 3.5 BER of QPSK (Quick Result) QPSK uses two independent BPSK channels on the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) rails. Since the noise components on I and Q are independent: $$P_b^{QPSK} = P_{b,\,I} + P_{b,\,Q} - P_{b,\,I}\,P_{b,\,Q} \approx 2P_{b,\,BPSK} \text{ (per channel)}$$ But because QPSK packs **2 bits/symbol**, the energy per bit is *half* the symbol energy, leading to: $$P_b^{QPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = P_b^{BPSK}$$ Same BER as BPSK, but **half the bandwidth** — this is the entire reason QPSK is preferred in bandwidth-limited applications like satellite and cellular. ## 3.6 Asymptotic BER Analysis (Waterfall Slope) At large $E_b/N_0$, the Q-function can be bounded by its Chernoff-style upper bound: $$Q(x) \le \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right)$$ Applying to BPSK with $x = \sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$: $$P_b^{BPSK} \le \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$ For BFSK-coherent with $x = \sqrt{E_b/N_0}$: $$P_b^{BFSK} \le \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$$ **Consequence (in dB):** Take the ratio of the exponents at large $E_b/N_0$. For the same target $P_b$, the difference in required $E_b/N_0$ is: $$\Delta\!\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{Exponent in BPSK}}{\text{Exponent in BFSK}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(2) = 3 \text{ dB}$$ This is the **rigorous proof** of the 3-dB rule. ## 3.7 M-ary Schemes (Brief) For completeness, the KTU syllabus expects you to **mention** (not necessarily derive) the following: - **M-PSK:** $P_b \approx \dfrac{2}{\log_2 M} Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \log_2 M \,\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$ - **M-FSK orthogonal (coherent):** $P_b \approx \dfrac{M/2}{\log_2 M} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\log_2 M \,\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ Both show that increasing $M$ reduces bandwidth at the cost of *higher* required $E_b/N_0$. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Digital Communication System ```mermaid flowchart LR Src[Source
Bits] --> Enc[Channel
Encoder] Enc --> Mod[Digital
Modulator
BPSK/QPSK/BFSK] Mod --> Tx[Transmit
Filter] Tx --> Ch[AWGN
Channel] Ch --> Rx[Receive
Filter] Rx --> Demod[Coherent or
Non-coherent
Demodulator] Demod --> Dec[Channel
Decoder] Dec --> Sink[Destination
Bits] Ch -.- Noise[Noise n t
PSD N0 by 2] Demod -.- DecRule[Decision
Threshold 0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The noise enters at the **AWGN Channel** block. The demodulator's decision threshold produces the bit errors that BER measures. The choice of modulation/detection in the middle two blocks determines the *shape* of the BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curve. ## 4.2 BER Performance Comparison — Schematic Plot ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "BER vs Eb by N0 in dB" dir1[Low Eb by N0 left side] dir2[Waterfall Region middle] dir3[High Eb by N0 right side] dir1 --> dir2 --> dir3 end P1[BPSK QPSK
Best Power

Half bandwidth of BPSK] ``` ## 4.4 Decision-Theory View (Geometric Distance) ```mermaid flowchart TB Sig1[Signal s0 plus root Eb] Sig2[Signal s1 minus root Eb] Thresh[Decision Threshold 0] Noise[Noise Spread sigma] Region0[Region R0 y greater than 0] Region1[Region R1 y less than 0] Sig1 --> Region0 Sig2 --> Region1 Thresh --> Region0 Thresh --> Region1 Noise -.- Region0 Noise -.- Region1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a sketch of BER vs $E_b/N_0$, always label the **asymptotic dB gap** between BPSK and BFSK as exactly **3 dB at high SNR**. This is what differentiates a full-mark answer from a partial one. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define Bit Error Rate (BER) and the metric $E_b/N_0$. Why is $E_b/N_0$ preferred over plain SNR for comparing digital modulation schemes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **BER (1 mark):** Bit Error Rate is the average probability that a transmitted bit is decoded incorrectly at the receiver due to noise and channel impairments. Mathematically: $$P_b = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N_{errors}}{N_{bits}}$$ - **$E_b/N_0$ definition (1 mark):** $E_b$ is the average transmitted energy per information bit, and $N_0$ is the two-sided noise power spectral density (in W/Hz) of the AWGN. $E_b/N_0$ is a **normalized, dimensionless** energy ratio expressed in dB as $10 \log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$. - **Why preferred (1 mark):** Plain SNR depends on the receiver's noise bandwidth and bit rate, which vary with the modulation scheme. $E_b/N_0$ normalizes out bandwidth and rate, giving a **fair, modulation-independent** metric for plotting performance curves. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Compare coherent and non-coherent detection of binary signals in terms of BER performance and receiver complexity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Coherent Detection | Non-Coherent Detection | |---|---|---| | BER performance | Better (e.g., BPSK ≈ $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$) | Worse (e.g., DPSK ≈ $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$, NC-BFSK ≈ $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$) | | Complexity | Requires carrier phase synchronization (PLL/Costas loop) | Simpler envelope or differential detector | | Use case | Bandwidth/power critical links (satellite, 4G) | Low-cost, burst-mode links (RFID, Bluetooth legacy) | | Typical gap | Reference | ~1–3 dB worse than coherent counterpart at high SNR | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write "non-coherent always loses 3 dB". This is wrong — the penalty is **3 dB for BFSK** (because of the square-root factor 2 → 1 in the Q-argument) but only **~1 dB for DPSK** vs BPSK. Examiners will deduct for over-generalization. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` #### (a) Derive the bit error probability expression for coherent BPSK over an AWGN channel. Sketch the BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curve and identify the waterfall region. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step model solution:** 1. **Signal model (1 mark):** $s_0 = +\sqrt{E_b}\phi(t)$ and $s_1 = -\sqrt{E_b}\phi(t)$, with $\phi(t)$ a unit-energy pulse over $[0, T_b]$. The received signal is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, with $n(t)$ AWGN of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. 2. **Correlator output (1 mark):** Projecting onto $\phi(t)$: $$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi(t)\,dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + z, \quad z \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$ 3. **Decision rule (1 mark):** The ML rule is $\hat{b} = 0$ if $y \ge 0$, else $\hat{b} = 1$. 4. **Error probability (2 marks):** Assuming $s_0$ sent, $P(e \mid s_0) = P(z < -\sqrt{E_b})$. Standardizing: $$P(e \mid s_0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ By symmetry, average BER: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. 5. **Curve description (1 mark):** As $E_b/N_0$ increases from 0 dB, the curve has a flat floor at $P_b = 0.5$ (left), a steep waterfall between ~3 and 10 dB, and an exponential tail beyond 10 dB approaching 0. 6. **Key numerical check (1 mark):** At $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB, $P_b \approx 10^{-5}$, which is the canonical operating point for uncoded BPSK voice links. #### (b) Plot and compare the BER vs $E_b/N_0$ performance of BPSK, QPSK, coherent BFSK, and DPSK. Discuss the **bandwidth-power trade-off** and the 3-dB gap. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step model solution:** 1. **Curves and their expressions (2 marks):** - BPSK: $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ - QPSK: $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical** to BPSK. - Coherent BFSK: $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ - DPSK: $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ 2. **Vertical-axis comparison at $E_b/N_0 = 8$ dB (1 mark):** BPSK/QPSK lowest, DPSK ~0.5 dB above, BFSK ~3 dB above. 3. **Asymptotic gaps (1 mark):** At high $E_b/N_0$, BPSK vs BFSK gap = $10\log_{10}(2) = 3$ dB; BPSK vs DPSK gap ≈ 1 dB; QPSK overlaps BPSK exactly. 4. **Bandwidth-power trade-off (2 marks):** - **BPSK** — bandwidth efficiency 0.5 bits/s/Hz, best power. - **QPSK** — same power as BPSK, 1 bit/s/Hz, **double the bandwidth efficiency** of BPSK. Used in satellite. - **BFSK** — power-inefficient (3 dB worse), bandwidth depends on tone spacing; useful where non-linearity is an issue. - **DPSK** — receiver simple (no PLL), ~1 dB power penalty, used in Bluetooth/legacy. 5. **Engineering recommendation (1 mark):** For a power-limited link → BPSK or QPSK. For a bandwidth-limited link → higher-order M-PSK (e.g., 8-PSK, 16-QAM). For low-cost bursts → DPSK or non-coherent BFSK. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative]` #### (a) Explain the Q-function and derive the BER of coherent orthogonal BFSK. Why is BFSK said to be 3-dB worse than BPSK at high SNR? **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step model solution:** 1. **Q-function definition (1 mark):** $$Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right)du = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$ 2. **BFSK signal model (1 mark):** $s_0 = \sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$, $s_1 = \sqrt{E_b}\phi_2(t)$, with $\int \phi_1\phi_2 = 0$. 3. **Two correlators and decision (2 marks):** $y_1, y_2$ are computed; decide $s_0$ if $y_1 > y_2$, else $s_1$. With $s_0$ sent, $y_1 = \sqrt{E_b} + z_1$ and $y_2 = z_2$, with $z_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ independent. 4. **Error derivation (2 marks):** $P(e \mid s_0) = P(y_2 > y_1) = P(z_2 - z_1 > \sqrt{E_b})$. Since $w = z_2 - z_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0)$, standardizing gives $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. 5. **3-dB gap explanation (1 mark):** Argument inside Q is $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ for BFSK vs $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ for BPSK. Setting $P_b$ equal: $$\sqrt{2(E_b/N_0)_{BPSK}} = \sqrt{(E_b/N_0)_{BFSK}} \;\Rightarrow\; (E_b/N_0)_{BFSK} = 2\,(E_b/N_0)_{BPSK}$$ i.e., **3 dB more energy** is required for BFSK at the same BER. #### (b) Discuss the asymptotic behavior of BPSK, QPSK, BFSK, and DPSK at high $E_b/N_0$. Compare their bandwidth requirements and the Shannon limit. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step model solution:** 1. **Asymptotic form of Q-function (1 mark):** $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$ for large $x$. 2. **BPSK / QPSK asymptotic (1 mark):** $P_b \approx \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi E_b/N_0}}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$. 3. **BFSK-coherent asymptotic (1 mark):** $P_b \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi E_b/N_0}}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ — half the exponent ⇒ **3-dB power loss**. 4. **DPSK asymptotic (1 mark):** $P_b \approx \frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ — same exponent as BPSK but pre-factor $1/2$ vs $1/(2\sqrt{\pi E_b/N_0})$, giving a small ~1 dB gap. 5. **Bandwidth comparison (1 mark):** BPSK = $0.5$ bit/s/Hz, QPSK = $1$ bit/s/Hz, BFSK ≈ $0.5$ bit/s/Hz (for minimum tone spacing), DPSK ≈ $0.5$–$1$ bit/s/Hz depending on pulse shaping. 6. **Shannon limit (1 mark):** The minimum $E_b/N_0$ for *any* reliable communication is $\ln 2 \approx -1.59$ dB. Coded systems approach this; uncoded BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB for $P_b = 10^{-5}$, giving a coding gap of ~11.2 dB. 7. **Engineering takeaway (1 mark):** Going from BPSK to QPSK gives a **bandwidth** win with no power cost; going to higher M-PSK or M-QAM gives *more* bandwidth win at *more* power cost — the central trade-off in modern cellular (LTE/5G) adaptive modulation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module-4 BER Plots):** > 1. **Forgetting to label the x-axis in dB** — examiners *will* deduct 0.5–1 mark for plots without unit labels. > 2. **Confusing the Q-argument for QPSK** — many students write $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ for QPSK. Correct answer is $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ (same as BPSK). The bandwidth efficiency, not the BER formula, is the difference. > 3. **Stating "BPSK is 3 dB better than DPSK"** — this is wrong. BPSK is only ~**1 dB** better than DPSK; BFSK is the scheme 3 dB worse than BPSK. > 4. **Forgetting to mention the BER floor at 0.5** for low $E_b/N_0$ — this is part of the *plot*, not just the formula. > 5. **Not stating the assumption of AWGN** — every BER formula is conditional on AWGN; on a fading channel, completely different expressions apply. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **BER definition:** Probability of a bit being decoded in error. Aka $P_b$ or $P_e$. In AWGN, it depends on the modulation scheme and $E_b/N_0$. - **$E_b/N_0$:** Energy-per-bit to noise-spectral-density. Always in dB for plots. Preferred over SNR for fair comparison. - **Q-function:** Tail probability of standard normal. $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$, $Q(-\infty) = 1$. $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. - **Master BER formulas (memorize these four):** - BPSK / QPSK (coherent): $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ - BFSK orthogonal (coherent): $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ - BFSK orthogonal (non-coherent): $P_b = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ - DPSK: $P_b = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ - **The 3-dB rule:** BPSK needs **half** the energy of BFSK-coh for the same $P_b$ at high SNR — that is, **3 dB less**. - **The 1-dB rule:** BPSK is only ~1 dB better than DPSK at high SNR, but DPSK needs no carrier phase recovery. - **BER floor:** $P_b \to 0.5$ as $E_b/N_0 \to -\infty$ dB for all binary schemes. This is the **information-theoretic limit** for binary antipodal detection under ML. - **Waterfall region:** Steep drop in BER between 3 and 12 dB for BPSK. Slope ≈ $-1$ decade per dB at high SNR (doubling $E_b/N_0$ reduces BER by ~factor of $e$). - **Bandwidth efficiency ranking (best to worst):** QPSK > BPSK ≈ BFSK > DPSK (depending on shaping). QPSK achieves BPSK BER at *half* the bandwidth. - **Shannon limit:** $-1.59$ dB is the absolute floor for reliable communication; coded systems approach it; uncoded schemes are 5–10 dB away. - **QPSK = BPSK in BER but better in bandwidth** — this is the single most-tested KTU fact in Module 4. - **Coherent > Non-coherent in BER**, but non-coherent is simpler — always a trade-off question. - **Asymptotic form** (use for $E_b/N_0 > 10$ dB): $$P_b \approx \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}, \quad x = \text{argument of Q}$$ - **Plot axes:** x-axis = $E_b/N_0$ in dB (typically 0 to 14 dB), y-axis = $P_b$ in log scale (typically $10^0$ to $10^{-8}$). - **Always label the curves** with their scheme names, and the dB gap on the asymptote for full marks. - **For M-PSK / M-FSK:** Higher $M$ ⇒ better bandwidth efficiency, worse power efficiency, and a steeper curve (more sensitive to noise). - **BER target reference points (memorize for numerical questions):** - $P_b = 10^{-5}$ for BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB. - $P_b = 10^{-4}$ for BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 8.4$ dB. - $P_b = 10^{-5}$ for BFSK-coh requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 12.6$ dB. - $P_b = 10^{-5}$ for DPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 10.3$ dB. - **Assumption reminder:** All formulas in this module are valid for the **AWGN** channel only. Fading channels are covered in advanced electives. - **Coding gain:** When FEC is applied, the entire curve shifts **leftward** by an amount equal to the code's coding gain (e.g., a rate-1/2 convolutional code with soft-decision Viterbi decoding gives ~5 dB coding gain at $P_b = 10^{-5}$).

topics.title: QPSK transmitter and receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT744 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QPSK transmitter and receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)** is a digital bandpass modulation technique in which the **phase** of a sinusoidal carrier is discretely varied among **four equally spaced values** (typically $0$, $\pi/2$, $\pi$, and $3\pi/2$ radians) according to the incoming binary data stream. Because four distinct phase states represent **two bits per symbol**, QPSK doubles the data rate of BPSK for the same bandwidth, achieving a spectral efficiency of **2 bits/second/Hz**.

Mathematically, the QPSK modulated signal during any symbol interval $T_s$ is expressed as:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

where $\theta_i \in \left\{0,\ \frac{\pi}{2},\ \pi,\ \frac{3\pi}{2}\right\}$ is the phase shift selected by the dibit (pair of bits), $E_s$ is the **energy per symbol**, and $T_s$ is the **symbol duration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** QPSK belongs to **M-ary PSK** modulation, where $M = 4$. The bits-to-symbol mapping uses **Gray coding** to ensure adjacent phases differ by only one bit, minimizing bit errors when a phase misidentification occurs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Compass and the Clock"

Imagine a **sailor steering a ship** using a compass. Instead of pointing the ship in just two directions (North or South as in BPSK), the sailor now has **four possible headings**:
- **North (0°)** = `00`
- **East (90°)** = `01`
- **South (180°)** = `11`
- **West (270°)** = `10`

Each heading carries **two pieces of information** (two bits) at once. The sailor makes a single heading choice per "decision interval" (symbol period $T_s$). On the receiving end, a compass reader identifies the heading, and since adjacent headings differ by only one bit (Gray-coded), even if the ship veers slightly into a neighboring quadrant, only **one bit** will be misinterpreted, not two. This is the elegance of QPSK — **double the information with the same transmission interval**.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Number of phases | $M$ | **4** |
| Bits per symbol | $k = \log_2 M$ | **2 bits/symbol** |
| Symbol duration | $T_s$ | **$2T_b$** (twice the bit duration) |
| Bandwidth efficiency | $\eta$ | **2 bits/s/Hz** |
| Carrier frequency | $f_c$ | Much greater than symbol rate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Signal Representation Using Orthogonal Basis Functions

The fundamental insight behind QPSK is the **decomposition into two orthogonal BPSK carriers**:
1. An **in-phase carrier**: $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{T_s}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
2. A **quadrature carrier**: $\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{T_s}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)$

Any QPSK signal can be written as:

$$
s_i(t) = \underbrace{s_{i1}}_{\text{I-component}} \, \phi_1(t) + \underbrace{s_{i2}}_{\text{Q-component}} \, \phi_2(t)
$$

For the four phase states, the I and Q components take values $\pm\sqrt{E_s/2}$:

| Dibit $b_1 b_0$ | Phase $\theta_i$ | I-component $s_{i1}$ | Q-component $s_{i2}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `00` | $0$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ |
| `01` | $\pi/2$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ |
| `11` | $\pi$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ |
| `10` | $3\pi/2$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The decomposition above reveals that **QPSK = two independent BPSK signals transmitted simultaneously** on orthogonal carriers. This is the "Why" behind QPSK's doubled bandwidth efficiency.

---

## 2.2 Bit-to-Symbol Mapping (Gray Coding)

The serial bit stream $b(t)$ is first converted into dibits using a **serial-to-parallel (S/P) converter**. The S/P block outputs two parallel streams:
- **Even bits** → I-channel
- **Odd bits** → Q-channel

Each stream has a bit rate of $R_b/2$ and uses a bit duration of $T_b$. However, since **two bits** form one symbol, the symbol duration becomes:

$$
T_s = 2T_b
$$

The I-channel bits modulate $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (a BPSK modulator) and the Q-channel bits modulate $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (another BPSK modulator). The two BPSK outputs are then **summed** to form the QPSK waveform.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $M = 2^k$, with $k=2$ | Number of M-ary symbols (4 for QPSK) |
| 2 | $T_s = k \cdot T_b = 2T_b$ | Symbol duration in terms of bit duration |
| 3 | $R_s = R_b / k = R_b / 2$ | Symbol rate (Baud) |
| 4 | $BW_{\min} = R_s = R_b/2$ | Minimum Nyquist bandwidth |
| 5 | $\eta = R_b / BW = 2$ | Spectral efficiency in bits/s/Hz |
| 6 | $E_b = E_s / k = E_s / 2$ | Energy per bit |
| 7 | $s_i(t) = \sqrt{\tfrac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i)$ | QPSK signal representation |
| 8 | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | **Probability of bit error** (same as BPSK!) |
| 9 | $P_s = 1 - \left[1 - Q\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)\right]^2$ | Probability of symbol error |
| 10 | $\theta_i = (2i - 1)\pi/4,\ i=1,2,3,4$ | Generic QPSK phase set |
| 11 | $s_{i1} = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i$ | I-channel symbol amplitude |
| 12 | $s_{i2} = \sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i$ | Q-channel symbol amplitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The probability of **bit error** $P_b$ for QPSK under AWGN with coherent detection is **identical** to BPSK. This is because QPSK can be viewed as two orthogonal BPSK channels of half the bit energy each, but combined they yield the same $P_b$ formula.

---

## 2.4 Why QPSK Matters in Real-World Engineering

QPSK strikes the optimal balance between **bandwidth efficiency** and **power efficiency**. It is the workhorse modulation scheme in:
- **Satellite communications** (DVB-S2, VSAT systems)
- **Cellular 4G LTE** (downlink control channels)
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** legacy modes
- **Cable modems** (DOCSIS)
- **RFID and NFC** systems
- **GPS** navigation signals

The reason is its **constant envelope** property and graceful degradation under noise — only $\approx 3$ dB more SNR is required than BPSK for the same $P_b$, while halving the required bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the QPSK Waveform

Starting with the generic M-ary PSK expression:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \left[\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\theta_i - \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\theta_i\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Define the two orthonormal basis functions:

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to express $s_i(t)$ as a linear combination:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i \cdot \phi_1(t) + \sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i \cdot \phi_2(t)
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the I and Q components:

$$
s_{i1} = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i, \quad s_{i2} = \sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i
$$

**Step 5:** For the four QPSK phases $\theta_i \in \{45°,\ 135°,\ 225°,\ 315°\}$ (offset QPSK variant), each component evaluates to $\pm\sqrt{E_s/2}$. For the standard phases $\{0°,\ 90°,\ 180°,\ 270°\}$, the same result holds:

$$
s_{i1}, s_{i2} \in \left\{+\sqrt{\tfrac{E_s}{2}},\ -\sqrt{\tfrac{E_s}{2}}\right\}
$$

> This derivation proves that **QPSK occupies two dimensions of signal space** with four distinct constellation points equally distant from the origin at distance $\sqrt{E_s}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Probability of Bit Error for QPSK

**Step 1:** Express QPSK as two independent BPSK signals. The received signal after coherent demodulation on the I-channel is:

$$
r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1 = \pm\sqrt{E_s/2} + n_1
$$

where $n_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ N_0/2)$.

**Step 2:** Apply the BPSK error probability formula. For a single BPSK channel with energy $E_s/2$ per bit, the probability of error is:

$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2(E_s/2)}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E_s = 2E_b$ (since $E_b = E_s/k = E_s/2$):

$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** For the symbol error rate (probability of confusing one of the four points), use the union bound for the I and Q channels:

$$
P_s = 1 - \left(1 - P_b\right)^2
$$

**Step 5:** For high SNR ($P_b \ll 1$), simplify using the approximation $(1-P_b)^2 \approx 1 - 2P_b$:

$$
P_s \approx 2 P_b = 2\, Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** When asked to "compare QPSK and BPSK error performance," emphasize that $P_b$ is **identical**, but the **bandwidth requirement halves**. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

---

## 3.3 Power Spectral Density & Bandwidth Computation

The baseband PSD of the QPSK I (or Q) channel is identical to that of BPSK:

$$
S_{bb}(f) = 2E_b \left(\frac{\sin 2\pi f T_b}{2\pi f T_b}\right)^{\!2}
$$

The passband PSD of QPSK (double-sided, with $T_s = 2T_b$):

$$
S_{QPSK}(f) = \frac{E_s}{2} \left[ \left(\frac{\sin \pi(f-f_c)T_s}{\pi(f-f_c)T_s}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\sin \pi(f+f_c)T_s}{\pi(f+f_c)T_s}\right)^{\!2} \right]
$$

The **null-to-null bandwidth** is:

$$
BW_{\text{null-null}} = \frac{2}{T_s} = \frac{1}{T_b} = R_b
$$

The **minimum Nyquist bandwidth** (rectangular pulse shaping) is:

$$
BW_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = R_s
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 QPSK Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["BINARY DATA b(t)"] --> B["SERIAL TO PARALLEL CONVERTER"]
    B --> C["I-CHANNEL bI EVEN BITS"]
    B --> D["Q-CHANNEL bQ ODD BITS"]
    C --> E["NRZ ENCODER +1 -1"]
    D --> F["NRZ ENCODER +1 -1"]
    E --> G["MULTIPLIER X COS 2 pi fc t"]
    F --> H["MULTIPLIER X SIN 2 pi fc t"]
    G --> I["SUMMER ADDER"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["QPSK TRANSMITTED SIGNAL s(t)"]
    G --> K["BANDPASS FILTER BPF"]
    H --> K
    K --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Flow Explanation:** The serial bit stream is split into two parallel streams (dibits). Each stream is NRZ-encoded into bipolar $\pm 1$ levels. The I-stream modulates a cosine carrier, the Q-stream modulates a sine carrier. The two BPSK-modulated signals are summed to produce the final QPSK waveform.

---

## 4.2 QPSK Receiver (Coherent Demodulator) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RECEIVED QPSK r(t) plus NOISE"] --> B["BANDPASS FILTER BPF"]
    B --> C["SPLITTER TO TWO CORRELATORS"]
    C --> D["MULTIPLY BY COS 2 pi fc t"]
    C --> E["MULTIPLY BY SIN 2 pi fc t"]
    D --> F["INTEGRATE OVER TS"]
    E --> G["INTEGRATE OVER TS"]
    F --> H["SAMPLE AT t EQUALS TS"]
    G --> I["SAMPLE AT t EQUALS TS"]
    H --> J["THRESHOLD DETECTOR ZERO LEVEL"]
    I --> K["THRESHOLD DETECTOR ZERO LEVEL"]
    J --> L["PARALLEL TO SERIAL CONVERTER"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["RECONSTRUCTED BINARY DATA b hat t"]
    H --> N["CARRIER PHASE RECOVERY LOOP"]
    E --> N
    N --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrier Recovery Requirement:** Coherent QPSK detection **requires** a phase-locked loop (PLL) or Costas loop at the receiver to regenerate the local carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$. A phase error of even a few degrees causes catastrophic inter-symbol interference between I and Q channels — this is the famous **"phase ambiguity"** problem that motivated the development of **Differential QPSK (DQPSK)**.

---

## 4.3 QPSK Signal Constellation (Geometric Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Constellation["QPSK Constellation Diagram IQ Plane"]
        direction TB
        P1["00 -- I plus -- Q minus BOTTOM RIGHT"] 
        P2["01 -- I minus -- Q plus TOP RIGHT"]
        P3["11 -- I minus -- Q minus TOP LEFT"]
        P4["10 -- I plus -- Q plus BOTTOM LEFT"]
    end
```

| Quadrant | I-component | Q-component | Dibit (Gray-coded) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| I (Bottom-Right) | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | `00` |
| II (Top-Right) | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | `01` |
| III (Top-Left) | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | `11` |
| IV (Bottom-Left) | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | `10` |

---

## 4.4 Generation Methods — Summary Architecture

**Method 1 — Direct Method (Phase Modulation):**
A single carrier oscillator passes through a phase modulator driven by the dibit. The phase modulator adds one of $\{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$.

**Method 2 — Selection Method:**
Four pre-computed carrier waveforms (one per phase) are stored in a ROM. The dibit acts as a 2-to-4 selector that picks the appropriate waveform from the ROM — a popular digital implementation.

**Method 3 — Composite Method (I/Q Modulation, Most Common in Practice):**
The dibit splits into I and Q streams, each undergoes BPSK modulation, and the two outputs are summed. This is the architecture shown in Section 4.1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define QPSK. Why is it called "quadrature"?**

**Model Answer:**
QPSK is a digital modulation scheme in which the phase of the carrier takes one of four equally spaced values — $0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,$ and $3\pi/2$ — to represent dibits (pairs of bits). It is called "quadrature" because it uses two orthogonal carriers — $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (in-phase) and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (quadrature, $90°$ shifted). The two channels are mathematically orthogonal, allowing them to be transmitted simultaneously without mutual interference.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of QPSK over BPSK.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Doubled bandwidth efficiency** — QPSK transmits 2 bits per symbol, halving the bandwidth required for the same bit rate.
2. **Same bit error performance** — $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$, identical to BPSK, so no SNR penalty per bit.
3. **Better suited for high-data-rate applications** — its symbol rate is half the bit rate, reducing ISI in bandlimited channels.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of QPSK signal. Derive the expression for the QPSK waveform.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above). Binary data $b(t)$ is split by a serial-to-parallel converter into I-channel bits $b_I(t)$ and Q-channel bits $b_Q(t)$, each at rate $R_b/2$.

**Step 2 — NRZ Encoding:** Each stream is converted to bipolar NRZ levels $d_I(t)$ and $d_Q(t)$, taking values $+1$ or $-1$.

**Step 3 — Carrier Modulation:**
$$
s_I(t) = d_I(t) \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$
$$
s_Q(t) = d_Q(t) \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4 — Summation:** The transmitted QPSK signal is:
$$
s(t) = s_I(t) + s_Q(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\left[d_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + d_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Compact form using symbol duration $T_s = 2T_b$:** Let $E_s = 2E_b$:
$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right)
$$
where $\theta_i = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d_Q(t)}{d_I(t)}\right)$ produces the four phases $\{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Draw the QPSK constellation diagram. Explain the role of Gray coding in minimizing bit errors.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constellation:** Four points are located on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ at angles $0°,\ 90°,\ 180°,\ 270°$. Each axis represents the I and Q components.

**Step 2 — Gray Coding Assignment:** Adjacent constellation points are assigned dibits that differ in only **one** bit. For example, going counterclockwise:
- $0° \to 00$
- $90° \to 01$ (differs in 1 bit)
- $180° \to 11$ (differs in 1 bit)
- $270° \to 10$ (differs in 1 bit)

**Step 3 — Error Mechanism:** When the received point (due to noise) crosses into an adjacent quadrant, the most likely error is a transition to the **nearest neighboring point**. Because of Gray coding, this neighbor differs by only **one bit**, ensuring the symbol error translates to only **one bit error** instead of two.

**Step 4 — Quantitative Impact:** Without Gray coding, a symbol error could cause up to 2 bit errors, doubling $P_b$. With Gray coding at high SNR:
$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{2} \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $E_s$ to $E_b$ using $E_b = E_s/2$ — this costs **2 marks** in derivation problems.
> 2. Drawing the constellation without axis labels (I and Q axes missing) — costs **1 mark**.
> 3. Confusing Gray coding with binary coding in the dibit assignment — the **adjacency** property is the key, not the numerical sequence.
> 4. Not mentioning the **serial-to-parallel converter** explicitly — examiners allocate **1 mark** for this component.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the coherent detection of QPSK signals with a block diagram. Derive the probability of error expression.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Receiver Front End:** The received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is bandpass-filtered and split into two parallel branches.

**Step 2 — I-branch correlation:** Multiply by locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrate over $T_s$:
$$
y_I = \int_0^{T_s} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_s/2} + n_I
$$

**Step 3 — Q-branch correlation:** Multiply by locally generated $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrate:
$$
y_Q = \int_0^{T_s} r(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_s/2} + n_Q
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Each integrator output is sampled at $t = T_s$ and passed through a threshold detector (decision at zero level) to recover $\hat{d}_I$ and $\hat{d}_Q$.

**Step 5 — P/S conversion:** The two parallel bit streams are recombined into a serial stream.

**Step 6 — Error Probability Derivation:**
Since $n_I$ and $n_Q$ are independent Gaussian with variance $N_0/2$, the I-branch SNR is:
$$
\text{SNR}_I = \frac{E_s/2}{N_0/2} = \frac{E_s}{N_0}
$$
The probability of bit error per branch is $Q(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}) = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

**Step 7 — Total $P_b$:** Since the I and Q decisions are independent and equally likely to error:
$$
\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A QPSK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 10$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. The received power is $P_r = 10\,\mu W$. Compute (i) the energy per bit $E_b$, (ii) the symbol duration $T_s$, and (iii) the probability of bit error $P_b$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy per bit:**
$$
E_b = \frac{P_r}{R_b} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{Joules} = 1\ \text{pJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Symbol duration:** Since $k = 2$ bits/symbol,
$$
T_s = \frac{k}{R_b} = \frac{2}{10^7} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 200\ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Bit error probability:**
Given $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz, so $N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ W/Hz.
$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0005
$$
$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{10^{-3}}\right) = Q(0.0316)
$$
Using the Q-function approximation $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{2} - \frac{x}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$ for small $x$:
$$
P_b \approx 0.5 - 0.0316 \times 0.3989 \approx 0.487
$$
This is unacceptably high, indicating the system is **power-limited** at this SNR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Problems:**
> 1. Mixing units — $P_r$ in watts, $R_b$ in bps, $N_0$ in W/Hz. Always normalize first.
> 2. Using $E_s$ instead of $E_b$ in the Q-function. The formula $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ **always** uses $E_b$.
> 3. Forgetting that $N_0/2$ is the **two-sided** PSD. The one-sided PSD $N_0$ in the formula equals **twice** the given two-sided value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- QPSK uses **4 phase states** to represent **2 bits per symbol**, giving **2 bits/s/Hz** spectral efficiency.
- The **symbol duration** $T_s = 2T_b$, so symbol rate = half of bit rate.
- QPSK = **two independent BPSK signals** on orthogonal cosine and sine carriers.
- **Probability of bit error** under coherent detection: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical** to BPSK.
- **Probability of symbol error**: $P_s \approx 2P_b$ at high SNR.
- **Gray coding** ensures adjacent constellation points differ by 1 bit, minimizing BER.
- **Bandwidth** (null-to-null) = $R_b$; minimum Nyquist bandwidth = $R_b/2$.
- **Required carrier phase accuracy** at receiver: typically within $\pm 5°$ to keep I/Q crosstalk below $-30$ dB.
- **QPSK vs BPSK trade-off:** Same $P_b$, half bandwidth — strict improvement.
- **Drawbacks:** Requires **coherent detection** with carrier recovery (PLL/Costas loop); vulnerable to phase ambiguity.
- **Variants:** Offset QPSK (OQPSK) for better nonlinearity tolerance; $\pi/4$-DQPSK for differential detection.
- **KTU Board exam focus areas:** Transmitter/receiver block diagrams, constellation drawing, $P_b$ derivation, comparison with BPSK, numerical problems on $E_b/N_0$.
